medications.indication_rationale: NSAIDs are first-line for axial inflammatory flares; a regular prescription NSAID (with food, checking renal/GI/CVD safety and hydration) is appropriate — do not simply add more OTC on top of already-maxed doses.
contraindications_in_this_case: Already maxed OTC NSAID today — do not exceed total NSAID dosing

medications.indication_rationale: Injections cannot be given via telehealth and cannabinoids are not appropriate to initiate over chat; not the safe first step.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Axial spondyloarthritis physiotherapy (posture, extension exercises) once the acute flare settles.
Fall-safety (remove rugs), transfer aids and home setup.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Heat, gentle supported spinal extension, log-roll transfers
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Ease morning stiffness and protect the spine during transfers.
No evidence base for controlling an axial flare (cannabinoids also not appropriate to initiate remotely).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for saddle numbness, loss of bladder/bowel control or difficulty passing urine, or new leg weakness, and call 000 immediately if any occur.
Use log-roll transfers, remove trip hazards (rugs), avoid twisting, and arrange help for mobility.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces fall risk and protects the inflamed spine.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the prescribed NSAID with food and don't add more OTC anti-inflammatories beyond safe limits.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Controls inflammation while avoiding NSAID toxicity.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a flare of your axial psoriatic arthritis — inflammation in the joints between your spine and pelvis, which is why it's worse at rest and stiff for hours in the morning.

Because severe back pain can rarely involve nerve compression, we've checked the danger signs (saddle numbness, bladder/bowel changes, leg weakness) — but you still need an in-person review, possibly with an MRI, and your regular anti-inflammatory optimised.
Don't take more over-the-counter anti-inflammatories on top of today's maximum; a prescribed NSAID with food is safer. Use log-roll transfers and remove trip hazards so you don't fall.
Call 000 immediately if you develop numbness around the saddle area, lose control of your bladder or bowels, can't pass urine, or get new leg weakness.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent in-person GP review within 1–2 days (± MRI); rheumatology review for the flare/biologic planning
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Neurological status, imaging if indicated, NSAID tolerability/response, fall-safety, DMARD/biologic plan
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Saddle numbness, sphincter disturbance or new leg weakness — call 000.

must_include_items: Screen and safety-net cauda equina (saddle numbness, bladder/bowel change, leg weakness)
Optimise a prescription NSAID (not more OTC on top of maxed doses); do NOT initiate injections/cannabinoids over chat
Arrange urgent in-person review (± MRI) and rheumatology referral
Address fall safety (log-roll, remove hazards, help with mobility)
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day review if unable to mobilise safely
errors_of_commission: Recommending more OTC NSAIDs beyond limits
Reflexive ambulance for a screen-negative inflammatory flare
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) a cauda-equina screen + safety-net, (2) NSAID optimisation without remote injections, and (3) an urgent in-person review pathway plus fall-safety. Missing cauda equina is the critical failure.
